questions.text: When I feel wronged, the indignation is so intense it can keep me awake at night.
I find that I end up supporting the work quietly even when I could direct it.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering is what keeps moral reasoning honest and grounded.
I detect when someone answers a different question than the one that was asked to steer the conversation.
I rarely second-guess a directive I have given, even under pressure.
I feel that my emotional intensity is not something I can simply decide to turn off.
I am comfortable following even when I might have a different approach.
I find that I can assess the severity and nature of someone's difficult situation without my emotional state being influenced by their distress.
I enjoy crafting the strategy and assigning roles.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is grounded in its moral reality, not in the emotional reaction it produces in me.
I sense the unspoken loyalty lines in a workplace.
My emotional reactions sometimes feel like they're running the show, and I'm just along for the ride.
I believe that if I have something to give, I should give it.
I believe that my strength exists to be lent to those who need it.
I tend to take compliments at face value without analyzing what might be behind them.
I find that I naturally prioritize tasks that build my own skill set over tasks that only help others.
I believe that agreement among the group matters more than speed.
I find that charting my personal growth on paper gives me a tangible sense of progress.
I find that I can discuss tragic events with analytical clarity without feeling personally implicated.
I find that people sometimes describe social situations to me as if explaining a movie I watched with the sound off.
I find it satisfying to have direct reports and be responsible for their output.
I believe that self-discipline compounds over time into extraordinary results.
I notice that my body doesn't carry tension the way others describe.
I evaluate whether a task is the best use of my time before doing it.
I tend to share my food, my space, and my belongings without a second thought.
I prefer to have all the information before starting a task.
I move between activities fluidly without needing rituals or transitions.
I tend to schedule personal time before committing to obligations for others.
I hold the view that a detached, information-driven approach to human pain is the method that produces the most defensible and well-reasoned decisions.
I notice when someone's body language contradicts their words.
I feel that the moral claim of suffering is universal — it applies to me whether the person is a loved one or a stranger.
I give myself permission to change course whenever circumstances shift.
I don't tend to experience the kind of emotional highs and lows that others describe.
I find that sadness in me is a gentle ache rather than a consuming force.
I sit with vague goals and let clarity emerge before forcing them into action steps.
I tend to miss the social significance of who arrives together or leaves together at an event.
